evidence: Packages map clearly to domain capabilities: api/, config/, exploration/, model/, query/, utils/, viz/, indexer/ — a new engineer can read the tree and explain what each subtree owns
model/package-info.java explicitly documents which types are DTOs vs model objects with derived accessors, and states that no I/O or HTTP concerns belong there
Handler classes in api/ are each focused on a subdomain: SketchHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, ConcordanceHandlers, CorpusQueryHandlers, VisualizationHandlers — each has a single, named HTTP concern
GrammarConfigSerializer lives in api/ (correct: it is an API-layer presenter for config data) and GrammarConfig/RelationConfig live in config/ (correct: pure data) — ownership is coherent

CLAUDE.md code-structure section still lists ~15 files that no longer exist: query/SemanticFieldExplorer.java, query/QueryResults.java, query/Token.java, query/TokenWindow.java, query/LemmaPredicate.java, query/TagPredicate.java, and others. SemanticFieldExplorer moved to exploration/, QueryResults moved to model/. A new engineer reading this doc would look in the wrong directories.

ExploreResponseAssemblerTest lives in src/test/java/.../model/ but imports and tests ExploreResponseAssembler from api/ — the test is in the wrong package subtree, obscuring where presentation tests should live

All api/ handler classes (ConcordanceHandlers, CorpusQueryHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, HttpApiUtils, RequestEntityTooLargeException, SketchHandlers, VisualizationHandlers) are package-private — consistent convention throughout the layer

All utility classes in utils/ (CqlUtils, MathUtils) and config/ (RelationUtils, RelationPatternBuilder) are declared 'public final class' with a private constructor — a consistent static-utility convention
LogDiceUtils in utils/ is 'public class' without 'final' — the only utility class in the codebase that breaks the utils/ final-class convention

ExploreResponseAssemblerTest is in the .../model/ test package yet imports and tests ExploreResponseAssembler from api/ — crosses the implicit convention that test classes shadow their source class's package

Records are used consistently for small options objects (ExplorationOptions, SingleSeedExplorationOptions, FetchExamplesOptions) and request parsing (BcqlRequest, ConcordanceExamplesRequest, ExplorationParams, ExecutedSketch) — no inconsistent POJO/builder mixing

@NonNull/@Nullable (jspecify) annotations are applied consistently on public API boundaries: QueryExecutor, ExploreResponseAssembler, SemanticFieldExplorer, and most public method parameters that have a documented nullability contract

api/ layer establishes a consistent contract: handler methods throw IllegalArgumentException (→ 400), RequestEntityTooLargeException (→ 413), and IOException (→ 500), all caught by HttpApiUtils.wrapWithErrorHandling — clear, single boundary
ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti validates seeds.size() < 2 and throws IllegalArgumentException — correct
ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldComparison also validates seeds.size() < 2 and throws IllegalArgumentException — consistent

CollocateProfileComparator.compareCollocateProfiles checks 'seedNouns == null || seedNouns.size() < 2' and returns ComparisonResult.empty() instead of throwing — the service layer silently returns empty for an invalid argument that the API layer treats as a 400

SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern(String,String,String,String,SingleSeedExplorationOptions) returns ExplorationResult.empty('') for an empty seed string rather than throwing — callers cannot distinguish 'valid query with no results' from 'invalid input silently swallowed'

CollocateQueryHelper constructor accepts @Nullable BlackLabIndex — production callers must always provide non-null but the constructor itself enforces nothing, shifting the failure to the first method call rather than construction time

query/ package mixes returns_null (8 occurrences) and throws (6 occurrences) across its public API — QueryExecutor.executeCollocations accepts @Nullable lemma and 'returns an empty list silently if null or empty' while other methods on the same interface throw on invalid input

GrammarConfigLoader.createDefaultEnglish() intentionally wraps IOException in IllegalStateException — the design rationale is documented inline, which is the right approach for a deliberate cross-layer exception translation
buildMultiSeedExplorationResult, buildCoreExploreResponse, buildDiscoveredNouns, buildCoreCollocates, identifyCommonCollocates — all name what they return, not how they work
exploreByPattern, compareCollocateProfiles, fetchExamples — verb+noun structure communicates intent and distinguishes the three exploration modes clearly
ExplorationParams, ConcordanceExamplesRequest, BcqlRequest, ExecutedSketch — record names are domain-vocabulary nouns, not generic 'Data' or 'Info' suffixes

RelationConfig.computeDeprel() is named with 'compute' but the method derives a value from existing record fields (pattern, id) with no external I/O — a 'derive' or 'extract' prefix would more accurately signal that this is a pure derivation: 'deriveDeprel()' or 'extractDeprel()'

parseRelationConfig in ExplorationHandlers does more than parse: it resolves aliases via RelationUtils.resolveRelationAlias, calls grammarConfig.getRelation, and validates that relationType is present. 'resolveAndValidateRelation' or 'resolveRelationConfig' would better communicate the compound nature of the operation

handleRelationsForType in SketchHandlers is named with 'handle' but it is a private implementation helper extracted from handleSurfaceRelations and handleDepRelations — the name 'handle' suggests it is itself a handler, not a helper. 'buildRelationsResponseForType' would be more accurate

QueryExecutor interface has one production implementation (BlackLabQueryExecutor) — documented in its Javadoc as intentional testability seam: 'A future split into narrower ports is feasible but deferred until a second implementation or clearer isolation need arises.' This is a considered, documented design choice.

SemanticFieldExplorer is documented as 'coordination facade for the HTTP exploration layer' — it owns single-seed exploration directly and delegates multi-seed to MultiSeedExplorer and comparison to CollocateProfileComparator. The facade is justified because it presents a stable API surface to HTTP handlers.

ExploreResponseAssembler exposes 6 public static methods (buildEdges x2, populateExploreResponse, formatSeedCollocates, formatDiscoveredNouns, formatCoreCollocates, formatAdjectiveProfile, populateComparisonResponse, serializeEdge) — formatSeedCollocates, formatDiscoveredNouns, formatCoreCollocates, and formatAdjectiveProfile are only called internally by the class and from tests. Making them public widens the surface beyond what callers need.

ExploreResponseAssembler.buildEdges(ExplorationResult) creates a cross-product of ALL seeds × ALL aggregate collocates as SEED_ADJ edges. In multi-seed mode, result.seedCollocates() is the aggregate union across seeds, so (seed B, collocate) edges are created even when collocate only appeared for seed A. The abstraction 'buildEdges(ExplorationResult)' assumes the ExplorationResult carries per-seed collocate attribution, but it does not — ExplorationResult stores only the aggregate.

HttpApiUtils co-locates response writing and request parameter parsing — documented in its Javadoc as intentional ('Both sets of methods serve a single concern — handling the HTTP layer'). The justification is reasonable and the class is package-private, so this is not a leakage issue.

FetchExamplesOptions is a record with a single int field — minimal wrapper. Its value is forward compatibility (binary compatibility for future fields) and it follows the documented options-object pattern. A bare int would also work but the record pays for itself through naming.

RelationUtils.findBestCollocatePattern performs a two-stage search (primaryTypes first, then any matching POS group) — this is appropriate parameterizable policy logic that avoids duplication across SemanticFieldExplorer and CollocateProfileComparator.

SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern is decomposed into four named phases (fetchSeedCollocates, buildCollocateToNounsMap, filterByMinShared, identifyCoreCollocates) with phase-header comments — this is a clear, intention-revealing decomposition for a non-trivial algorithm

CollocateProfileComparator.buildAdjectiveProfileList correctly annotates why two passes are needed: 'Second pass for variance (requires the mean — unavoidable two-pass for numerical stability)' — not a restating comment
CqlUtils.splitCqlTokens handles nested brackets and quoted strings in a single documented pass; the bracket-tracking logic is inherently complex but the code is as direct as the problem allows

VisualizationHandlers.handleVisualRadial uses explicit null checks for 'width' and 'height': 'int width = (obj.get("width") == null) ? 840 : obj.getIntValue("width")' — fastjson2's JSONObject.getIntValue(key, defaultValue) overload handles this in one call without the null check

SketchHandlers.handleSketchRequest performs manual URL path routing via string split and array index checks inside a handler method: 'parts.length > 1 && "dep".equals(parts[1])' — dispatching to handleDependencyRelationQuery vs handleFullDependencySketch. This is a mini-router embedded in application logic; fragile if paths change.

ExploreResponseAssembler.buildEdges(ExplorationResult) has a double loop that creates (seed, collocate) SEED_ADJ edges for every combination of seed × aggregate collocates — in multi-seed mode this produces spurious edges as the aggregate includes collocates not found for a given seed (see abstraction_fitness af_build_edges_cross_product for the full analysis)

CollocateProfileComparator.buildRawAdjectiveProfiles contains 'if (!adjectives.isEmpty() && logger.isDebugEnabled())' — the isDebugEnabled() guard is redundant since SLF4J's logger.debug() already gates on debug level internally; the guard was added to avoid subList object allocation but this is premature micro-optimization

ExplorationHandlers → SemanticFieldExplorer → MultiSeedExplorer / CollocateProfileComparator is a clean delegation chain: each layer receives exactly what it needs and returns a typed result object (ExplorationResult, ComparisonResult)
GrammarConfig → GrammarConfigLoader seam is clean: the loader produces an immutable GrammarConfig; callers only need GrammarConfig and never carry a loader reference

RelationConfig → RelationPatternBuilder seam is clean: the config record is a pure data carrier; all pattern-building logic is in the separate builder class, eliminating mutation or side effects at the data layer

ExploreResponseAssembler.buildEdges(ExplorationResult) in multi-seed mode creates SEED_ADJ edges for every (seed, aggregate_collocate) combination — the aggregate seedCollocates map in ExplorationResult is a union across all seeds, so a collocate found only for seed A is attributed to seed B as well. The root cause is that ExplorationResult does not carry per-seed collocate data, only the aggregate. The assembler cannot correctly attribute collocates to their source seeds without this data.

ExploreResponseAssembler mutation pattern: populateExploreResponse and populateComparisonResponse accept a Map<String,Object> and mutate it rather than returning a new map. The callers (ExplorationHandlers) construct the map, pass it through, then send it — the construction and population are split across two locations. This is an accepted T4-deferred pattern but it does create a seam where the map must be constructed in the right pre-populated state before calling populate*.

ConcordanceHandlers internally renames HTTP parameter names: 'seed' → 'noun' and 'collocate' → 'adjective' in parseConcordanceExamplesRequest. The rename is documented in the ConcordanceExamplesRequest Javadoc, and the translation happens at a single explicit boundary — this is the right pattern.
WordSketchApiServer wires all handlers via constructor injection with a clear two-constructor design (convenience constructor and full constructor) — dependency wiring is explicit and testable
SemanticFieldExplorer Javadoc includes a worked example with input/output trace for 'house' exploration — this is substantive documentation of a non-trivial algorithm, not boilerplate
RelationConfig.computeDeprel() Javadoc contains a design note explaining @Nullable vs Optional rationale — this adds genuine insight about a non-obvious design decision
GrammarConfigLoader.createDefaultEnglish() Javadoc documents a deliberate two-tier exception contract with a stated rationale — this is substantive

FetchExamplesOptions Javadoc: 'Follows the options-object pattern used by sibling types (ExplorationOptions, SingleSeedExplorationOptions) so that callers can extend the parameter set without breaking binary compatibility.' — for a one-field record, this explanation is appropriate and adds value

LogDiceUtils class-level Javadoc: 'Utility class for computing association scores for collocation analysis. Supported measures: - logDice: Symmetric association measure, range 0-14. Formula: ...' — the formula documentation is valuable; the 'Supported measures: - logDice:' list framing suggests a placeholder for future measures that never materialized. The list structure with one item is a mild AI-generative artifact.

ExplorationOptions record Javadoc comments: '/** Maximum collocates to retrieve per seed in the first lookup pass. */' and '/** Minimum logDice score threshold; collocates below this value are discarded. */' — these add value for the non-obvious parameters, but '/** Minimum number of shared collocates required for a noun to be included in results. */' on minShared is borderline — the field name already communicates this

CollocateProfileComparator private method logTopProfiles has two separate stream pipelines for shared and unshared profiles — both are used only for debug logging. The method itself is fine; splitting the two loops is slightly over-engineered for a debug-only helper
WordSketchApiServer Javadoc class comment lists all endpoints with full URL and HTTP method — this is genuinely useful operational documentation, not bloat
No references to 'LogDiceCalculator' anywhere in src/ — rename to LogDiceUtils is complete
No references to 'buildBaseExploreResponse' anywhere in src/ — rename to buildCoreExploreResponse is complete
No references to 'computeCollocateIntersection' anywhere in src/ — rename to buildMultiSeedExplorationResult is complete
No references to 'executeAndFormatCollocations' anywhere in src/ — rename to buildSketch is complete
No references to 'top_requested' anywhere in src/ — API consistency change (top_requested → top in ConcordanceHandlers response) is complete
HitRecord.collocateLemma in CollocateQueryHelper is declared '@Nullable String collocateLemma' in the record — the @Nullable annotation migration is complete
ExplorationOptions, SingleSeedExplorationOptions, FetchExamplesOptions all live in model/ — the move from wherever they were is complete
WordSketchApiServer.registerRoutes() has the legacy /api/semantic-field route wrapped in a /* @deprecated ... */ block comment — the comment-to-@deprecated-block migration is complete
No @link references to api/ classes found in model/ or exploration/ Javadocs — the api/ @link coupling removal is complete
ExplorationHandlersTest and VisualizationHandlersTest exist in the test tree — the new test additions are in place

CLAUDE.md code structure section still lists the pre-migration file layout: query/SemanticFieldExplorer.java, query/QueryResults.java, query/CQLVerifier.java, api/PatternSubstitution.java, and many predicate classes that do not exist — this is the one remaining artifact of the class-move migrations that was not applied to the developer documentation
incomplete_migration.issues_preventing_higher_score: CLAUDE.md code structure is the only remaining migration artifact. All code-level migrations are complete and verified.

evidence: api/(10 files, 1546 LOC): HTTP handlers, server, CORS/routing utilities — all serving the HTTP layer
config/(5 files): grammar loading only; no HTTP or query concerns leak in
exploration/(3 files): algorithms for single-seed, multi-seed, and comparison — cohesive
model/(15 files): pure DTOs and value objects; package-info.java explicitly documents the boundary
query/(4 files): query executor interface and BlackLab implementation — tight single concern
utils/(3 files): stateless helpers with no application-layer dependencies

ExploreResponseAssembler lives in api/ but its test is at src/test/java/.../model/ExploreResponseAssemblerTest.java — the test is placed one package down from where the class lives, reflecting navigational uncertainty about whether the assembler belongs in api/ or model/
package_organization.issues_preventing_higher_score: ExploreResponseAssembler in api/ has no HTTP imports; its test lives in the model/ test package, which signals the class straddles the api/model boundary. A developer looking for response-assembly logic would need to check api/ rather than the more intuitive model/ location.

evidence: ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldComparison Javadoc: 'requires exactly 2 comma-separated seeds values to form a meaningful pairwise comparison' — guard is seeds.size() < 2, which passes 3+ seeds through silently
CollocateQueryHelper constructor annotates BlackLabIndex as @Nullable but Javadoc says 'Production callers must always supply a non-null index' — annotation and prose contract disagree
QueryExecutor.executeCollocations declares @Nullable lemma and the implementation silently returns empty list — annotation matches behavior accurately
GrammarConfigLoader.createDefaultEnglish() wraps IOException into IllegalStateException, documented with rationale — intentional and consistent
LogDiceUtils.compute throws IAE on zero/negative inputs; callers guard with headwordFreq > 0 check before invoking — IAE is accurate but callers treat it as unreachable
RelationConfig.computeDeprel() returns @Nullable with documented rationale contrasting with Optional — consistent with its usage

SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern is structured as 4 named phases (fetchSeedCollocates, buildCollocateToNounsMap, filterByMinShared, identifyCoreCollocates) each in its own private method with a clear phase comment
CollocateQueryHelper.executeBcqlQuery separates hit collection (phase 1) and scoring (phase 2) into focused private methods with documented purposes
SketchHandlers.handleSketchRequest dispatches via guard-clause-first pattern: dep branch handled early, then single/full relation
MultiSeedExplorer delegates to four named private methods whose names accurately describe what they do

scoreHits in CollocateQueryHelper recomputes a limit from records.size() that was already bounded to sampleSize in executeBcqlQuery — the limit variable is always equal to records.size() and the Math.min is redundant
jspecify @NonNull/@Nullable annotations applied consistently on all public API methods in api/, query/, config/ packages
Optional<RelationType> used throughout RelationConfig to represent absent relation type — avoids a nullable Optional anti-pattern
Sealed interface ConcordanceResult (SnippetResult | CollocateResult) enables type-safe exhaustive pattern matching
CollocateQueryHelper(@Nullable BlackLabIndex index) — @Nullable on a production-always-non-null parameter creates a misleading type contract

ExplorationResult fields (seeds, seedCollocates, discoveredNouns, coreCollocates) have no @NonNull annotations despite the Javadoc guaranteeing 'never null, may be empty' for all collections — inconsistent with jspecify usage elsewhere in model/
GrammarConfig.getConfigPath() correctly annotated @Nullable; GrammarConfig.getVersion() correctly @NonNull
CollocateQueryHelper.HitRecord.collocateLemma recently annotated @Nullable — accurate
ExplorationHandlers private helpers parseRelationConfig and parseExplorationParams cleanly separate HTTP parsing from business logic
SemanticFieldExplorer facade delegates single-seed, multi-seed, and comparison to dedicated algorithm classes — each with a single focused responsibility
SketchHandlers.executeRelationQueries abstracts the loop-over-relations with a BiFunction builder callback, removing two near-identical loops

AdjectiveProfile record takes 10 positional constructor arguments (adjective, nounScores, presentInCount, totalNouns, avgLogDice, maxLogDice, minLogDice, variance, commonalityScore, distinctivenessScore) — at the call site in buildAdjectiveProfileList, seven of them are double literals with no label
buildCoreExploreResponse in ExplorationHandlers accepts 'Map<String, Object> extraParams' for open-ended response augmentation — currently used for only one key (nouns_per)
model/ has zero outbound imports to any other application package — clean base layer
utils/ imports nothing from the application — stateless helpers only
config/ imports from model/ and utils/ only — no api/ or query/ imports
exploration/ imports from config/, model/, and query/ — never from api/
api/ imports from exploration/, config/, model/, query/, and utils/ — appropriate top-layer aggregation
query/ imports from model/ and utils/ — no api/ or config/ imports
ExploreResponseAssembler in api/ imports from model/ and utils/ only — correctly does not import from exploration/ or config/
ExploreResponseAssemblerTest.java is placed in the model/ test package but imports ExploreResponseAssembler from api/ — test-level inversion of the dependency direction
cross_module_architecture.issues_preventing_higher_score: ExploreResponseAssemblerTest lives in the model/ test package while the class under test is in api/ production code. This test-level boundary inversion is a symptom of uncertain ownership. In production the dependency graph is clean; in the test tree there is a single cross-boundary reference that signals the class placement is not fully settled.

evidence: WordSketchApiServer constructor comment: 'Port binding is deferred to start() so that construction and network binding are separated' — I/O is fully deferred from construction
HttpApiUtils.setCorsHeader reads System.getProperty at call time, not at class load — per-test overrides work without class reload
GrammarConfigLoader.createDefaultEnglish() reads System.getProperty at call time — no class-level side effect
BlackLabQueryExecutor constructor opens the BlackLab index (file I/O) and declares throws IOException — Javadoc justifies this as 'inherent to BlackLab lifecycle model'
BlackLabSnippetParser static final Pattern constants are class-load-time initializations of pure regex values — no environment reads, no side effects
No global mutable state beyond SLF4J Logger instances (standard JVM idiom, stateless per class)
initialization_coupling.issues_preventing_higher_score: BlackLabQueryExecutor opens the BlackLab index in its constructor. This is explicitly justified and documented, but it means construction always implies I/O. The workaround (CollocateQueryHelper accepting @Nullable index for test subclasses) creates a production/test contract mismatch. A factory method or an open/close lifecycle split would eliminate the coupling without changing the documented intent.

evidence: SemanticFieldExplorerTest, MultiSeedExplorerTest, SemanticFieldExplorerInternalTest, CollocateProfileComparatorTest — full algorithm coverage with stub executors
GrammarConfigLoaderTest, RelationsValidationTest, RelationUtilsTest — config loading and validation covered
ExplorationHandlersTest (3 tests: happy-path for explore, explore-multi, compare) added recently
VisualizationHandlersTest (4 tests: mode error, body too large, invalid JSON, successful render)
HandlersTest covers error paths for SketchHandlers (missing lemma → 400) and ConcordanceHandlers (missing params → 400)
SketchHandlers has no happy-path unit tests — full sketch response shape, single-relation sketch, dep sketch response format are all untested at the handler level
ConcordanceHandlers.handleConcordanceExamples has only error-path tests in HandlersTest; no test verifies the response body structure for a valid request
ExplorationHandlersTest.handleSemanticFieldComparison_twoSeeds_returns200 only asserts body != null — no verification of response keys (adjectives, seeds, parameters)
BlackLab live-index tests (BlackLabQueryExecutorIntegrationTest, HybridAlgorithmTest, DependencySketchTest) are @Disabled — documented as wontfix
All success responses include 'status': 'ok' via direct put or via sendJsonResponse
All error responses return '{"error": "..."}' via HttpApiUtils.sendError with consistent HTTP codes (400, 413, 500)
explore and explore-multi both echo request parameters in a 'parameters' sub-map; compare does not include 'relation_type' in response (intentional, cross-relational)
All count fields follow the '{name}_count' suffix convention: seed_collocates_count, discovered_nouns_count, adjectives_count, edges_count

ConcordanceHandlers /api/concordance/examples response uses keys 'seed'/'collocate' matching HTTP param names; /api/semantic-field/examples response uses 'adjective'/'noun' matching its HTTP param names — two example endpoints use different vocabulary for analogous subject/collocate roles
/api/semantic-field/compare response omits 'relation_type' key present in explore and explore-multi responses
BCQL /api/bcql response echoes 'top' (previously 'top_requested') matching the request parameter name — consistent
WordSketchApiServer Javadoc: 'All endpoints are unauthenticated — access control is provided by the host firewall and the loopback-only bind'
Server binds exclusively to InetAddress.getLoopbackAddress() (127.0.0.1) — no external network interface exposure
Javadoc includes a deployment warning: 'Any change to the bind address must be accompanied by an authentication or network-level access-control layer'
All 13 registered routes use the same pattern: registerGetHandler/registerPostHandler → registerHandler → CORS + method check only, no auth
CORS origin is configurable via System.getProperty('cors.allow.origin') defaulting to 'http://localhost:3000' — the default is appropriately restrictive for local use
Body size limits (65536 bytes) and BCQL complexity limits (1024 chars, 20 bracket tokens) are uniformly applied to POST endpoints
authorization_consistency.issues_preventing_higher_score: The CORS allow-origin defaults to 'http://localhost:3000' via a system property override mechanism. A container deployment that changes the bind address but forgets to set cors.allow.origin gets a default that may be too permissive for the new environment. The security model document in the Javadoc warns about bind address changes but does not mention the CORS default as a related concern.

strengths: Package decomposition is genuinely domain-aligned: api/, config/, exploration/, model/, query/, utils/, viz/ each have a clear, single-sentence mandate
model/package-info.java explicitly governs what belongs in the model package, preventing boundary drift
Handler class split in api/ (Sketch/Exploration/Concordance/Visualization/CorpusQuery) means each HTTP subdomain has a named owner — change locality is good
GrammarConfig/GrammarConfigLoader separation correctly isolates file-IO from the data object callers depend on
RelationConfig record cleanly separates data from pattern-building logic (RelationPatternBuilder), demonstrating consistent decomposition style across the config subsystem
high_level_elegance.issue_character: Issues are documentation drift rather than structural problems: the codebase has evolved correctly but the CLAUDE.md structure map was not updated, and one test class is filed in the wrong subpackage.
high_level_elegance.score_rationale: The live code is well-decomposed and ownership is predictable — this is solidly above a 'significant drag' (60) baseline. The main deduction is that project contracts (CLAUDE.md structure section) have drifted from reality: a new engineer using that doc would look in the wrong directories for core classes. That plus the misplaced test bring it to 76 rather than 82+, where it would sit if docs were trustworthy.

strengths: Package-private access modifier on all api/ handler classes is applied uniformly — no accidental public exposure
'public final class' + private constructor convention for static utility classes is respected by CqlUtils, MathUtils, RelationUtils, RelationPatternBuilder
Record type usage for options/request objects is consistent across all handler and exploration classes
@NonNull/@Nullable annotations are applied at API-boundary methods throughout, not just in a few files
convention_outlier.issue_character: Two isolated outliers — one missing 'final' on a utility class and one misplaced test — rather than a systemic convention problem.
convention_outlier.score_rationale: The codebase maintains its conventions very well across a large number of files. The two outliers (LogDiceUtils missing 'final', ExploreResponseAssemblerTest in wrong package) are clearly inadvertent omissions rather than deliberate deviations, and they are limited in scope. A score of 77 reflects that the dominant pattern is solid but the outliers are observable enough that a code reviewer would notice them immediately.

strengths: The HTTP boundary has a single, uniform error strategy: IllegalArgumentException → 400, RequestEntityTooLargeException → 413, IOException → 500, implemented once in HttpApiUtils.wrapWithErrorHandling
GrammarConfigLoader.createDefaultEnglish() documents its IOException → IllegalStateException translation with a rationale comment explaining the two-tier exception contract
ExplorationHandlers validates seeds cardinality with IllegalArgumentException in both the multi-seed and comparison handlers consistently
error_consistency.issue_character: The service layer (exploration/) uses a silent-empty-return pattern for invalid inputs that the API layer treats as 400s, creating a gap where bad inputs can be swallowed before the error boundary.
error_consistency.score_rationale: The api/ boundary is consistent and well-designed. The drag comes from the service layer: CollocateProfileComparator silently returns empty for a null/undersized seed set instead of throwing, and SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern silently returns empty for a blank seed. These two cases allow invalid inputs to produce misleading empty-success responses when callers do not pre-validate. This is a meaningful inconsistency because the API layer does validate these inputs, but if the service layer is ever called directly (as in tests), errors are silently swallowed. This brings the score to 66 rather than 75+.

strengths: Exploration method names clearly distinguish the three modes: exploreByPattern (single-seed), exploreMultiSeed (multi-seed), compareCollocateProfiles (comparison)

Recent renames (buildBaseExploreResponse → buildCoreExploreResponse, computeCollocateIntersection → buildMultiSeedExplorationResult, executeAndFormatCollocations → buildSketch) have materially improved intent communication
Record type names for request/result objects use domain vocabulary throughout (ExplorationParams, ConcordanceExamplesRequest, ExecutedSketch) rather than generic suffixes
Phase-separation naming in SemanticFieldExplorer (fetchSeedCollocates, buildCollocateToNounsMap, filterByMinShared, identifyCoreCollocates) reflects the algorithm phases clearly
naming_quality.issue_character: Three isolated naming imprecisions — compute vs derive, parse vs resolve, handle vs build — each in a different file, indicating occasional inconsistency rather than a systemic naming problem.
naming_quality.score_rationale: Naming is a strength overall: domain vocabulary is used consistently, recent renames have fixed the most egregious cases, and the exploration API names are particularly clear. The three issues found are all minor mismatches between name and behavior (calling a derivation 'compute', calling a resolution 'parse', calling a builder 'handle'). These would cause brief confusion for a new reader but are not misleading in a harmful way. Score of 80 reflects a solid codebase with a small number of fixable imprecisions.

strengths: SemanticFieldExplorer-as-facade is explicitly designed and documented, providing a stable surface for HTTP handlers without exposing algorithm details
QueryExecutor interface is documented as a testability seam with a clear statement about when a port split would be warranted — this is honest about the abstraction's limits

RelationUtils.findBestCollocatePattern is a real abstraction that eliminates a duplicated two-stage lookup pattern that would otherwise appear in both SemanticFieldExplorer and CollocateProfileComparator
MultiSeedExplorer and CollocateProfileComparator are package-private algorithm classes — properly hidden behind SemanticFieldExplorer's facade
abstraction_fitness.issue_character: The main issue is a single design gap at the ExplorationResult/ExploreResponseAssembler seam: the assembler assumes per-seed collocate data that the result model does not carry, causing it to create spurious cross-product edges in multi-seed mode.
abstraction_fitness.score_rationale: Most abstractions in this codebase pay for themselves clearly — the facade pattern in SemanticFieldExplorer, the testability seam in QueryExecutor, and the static utility pattern in RelationUtils are all justified. The drag comes from one real seam gap (ExploreResponseAssembler.buildEdges relies on per-seed data that ExplorationResult does not carry) and one over-publication (internal format methods on ExploreResponseAssembler made public unnecessarily). A score of 72 reflects a codebase that is mostly well-abstracted with one structural gap.

strengths: Algorithm phase decomposition in SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern is clean and each phase method has a single, named responsibility
CollocateProfileComparator.buildAdjectiveProfileList correctly performs the two-pass mean/variance computation and documents why two passes are necessary
CqlUtils.splitCqlTokens handles a genuinely complex grammar without unnecessary abstraction layers
Most method bodies are appropriately sized — no 100-line God methods observed in the seed files
low_level_elegance.issue_character: Issues are scattered micro-imprecisions (unnecessary null-check where a default-value API exists, a manual URL router inside a handler, a redundant logger guard) rather than any systemic low-level problem.
low_level_elegance.score_rationale: Function internals are generally direct and well-organized. The main drag comes from three things: manual path-routing inside handleSketchRequest (brittle, hard to extend), VisualizationHandlers null-check pattern that bypasses the available API, and the redundant isDebugEnabled guard. None of these cause incorrect behavior, but they add noise and reduce the 'precision without defensive sprawl' criterion. Score of 74 reflects solid-but-imprecise internals.

strengths: The delegation chain ExplorationHandlers → SemanticFieldExplorer → MultiSeedExplorer is explicit and each boundary carries only the typed data the receiver needs
GrammarConfig/GrammarConfigLoader separation ensures callers depend only on the data, never on the loading machinery
HTTP parameter renaming (seed→noun, collocate→adjective) happens at a single documented seam in parseConcordanceExamplesRequest — the translation is centralized and visible
WordSketchApiServer constructor injection makes the dependency graph of the API layer transparent
mid_level_elegance.issue_character: The primary issue is a structural gap at the ExplorationResult/ExploreResponseAssembler seam: the result model was not extended to carry per-seed attribution when multi-seed was added, leaving the assembler unable to produce accurate seed-specific edges.
mid_level_elegance.score_rationale: Most seams in this codebase are clean — data flows in one direction, translation happens at named points, and collaborator wiring is explicit. The score of 70 reflects one real structural gap: the ExplorationResult model and the ExploreResponseAssembler are mismatched at the multi-seed edge-building seam, and the mutation-based populate* pattern (T4 deferred) means response construction is split across two call sites. These are not catastrophic, but they are the kind of seam issue that leads to subtle bugs and confusion when extending multi-seed visualizations.

strengths: SemanticFieldExplorer has a substantive worked-example Javadoc that explains a non-trivial algorithm — this is the opposite of restating comments
Design-rationale comments (RelationConfig @Nullable vs Optional, GrammarConfigLoader exception contract) add genuine insight that code alone cannot convey
No entry/exit logging on every function; debug logging is targeted to meaningful algorithm phases only
No defensive null-checks on values that cannot be null: @NonNull annotations are used where appropriate and checks are not doubled up
ai_generated_debt.issue_character: One mild formatting artifact in LogDiceUtils (single-item 'supported measures' list implying extensibility that doesn't exist) — isolated, not systemic.
ai_generated_debt.score_rationale: This codebase shows few AI-generated-debt hallmarks. Documentation is substantive rather than restating; debug logging is appropriately sparse; no defensive overengineering was observed. The one clear case is the LogDiceUtils 'Supported measures: - logDice:' list structure, which reads as a placeholder for a future list that stayed at one item. This mild artifact brings the score to 79 rather than 85+.

strengths: All named code-level renames (LogDiceCalculator, buildBaseExploreResponse, computeCollocateIntersection, executeAndFormatCollocations) are fully complete with no stale call sites
The @Nullable annotation on HitRecord.collocateLemma and the api/ @link decoupling in model/exploration/ Javadocs are both complete
The legacy route @deprecated block comment migration in WordSketchApiServer is done correctly
The model/ package move for ExplorationOptions, SingleSeedExplorationOptions, FetchExamplesOptions is complete and no stale imports were found
incomplete_migration.issue_character: The one remaining incomplete item is in developer documentation (CLAUDE.md), not in code — all code-level migrations are verified complete.
incomplete_migration.score_rationale: Code migrations are complete. The score stops at 82 rather than 90+ because CLAUDE.md still lists the pre-migration file structure (15+ non-existent files), which means the developer docs have not been updated to reflect the completed migrations. This is the same stale-docs gap identified in batch-1, and it is the sole evidence of incompleteness here.

strengths: All top-level packages have a single, clear sentence of purpose; api/, config/, exploration/, model/, query/, utils/ each own exactly one concern
ExplorationOptions, SingleSeedExplorationOptions, FetchExamplesOptions are correctly placed in model/ rather than exploration/, reflecting their nature as data carriers not algorithm classes
model/ has no outbound dependencies — a clean base layer any package can import without risk of cycles
utils/ contains only three stateless helpers (CqlUtils, LogDiceUtils, MathUtils) with no application-layer imports
indexer/blacklab/ is correctly nested under a two-level path that names both the concern (indexer) and the backend (blacklab)
package_organization.issue_character: A single structural outlier: ExploreResponseAssembler sits in api/ without HTTP dependencies, and its test is placed in the model/ test tree — an isolated placement ambiguity rather than a systemic organisation problem.
package_organization.score_rationale: The package graph is coherent and navigable end-to-end. A developer can predict where to find almost any class from the directory name alone. The one friction point — ExploreResponseAssembler's cross-boundary test placement — is a minor outlier that slightly undermines confidence in the api/model boundary. This is consistent with the 90 anchor (strong, minor friction) rather than 80 (recurring mismatches). Scored 87 because the misplacement is real evidence but the overall structure is genuinely strong.

strengths: QueryExecutor.executeCollocations: @Nullable lemma annotation accurately reflects the implementation's silent-empty-return behaviour, documented in the Javadoc
GrammarConfigLoader.createDefaultEnglish() documents exactly why it wraps IOException into IllegalStateException, so callers are never surprised
RelationConfig.computeDeprel() explicitly justifies @Nullable vs Optional with a design note, making the contract transparent
ExplorationResult.empty(String) enforces its @NonNull contract via Objects.requireNonNull at the entry point
LogDiceUtils.compute throws IAE on invalid inputs with specific argument names in the message — precise failure contract
contract_coherence.issue_character: Two isolated annotation/documentation mismatches: one where prose says 'exactly N' but the guard allows more, and one where @Nullable on a constructor parameter contradicts the Javadoc's production-caller requirement.
contract_coherence.score_rationale: The codebase has strong contract documentation habits and most APIs do what their signatures say. The two defects are discrete and not systemic — they appear in different classes for different reasons. A developer reading either would be briefly confused before the code resolves the ambiguity. This is above the 80 anchor (recurring friction) but below 90 (minor isolated issues with low downstream impact) because the @Nullable mismatch on a core helper constructor is the kind of issue that silently allows test doubles that mask production-only failure modes. Scored 82.

strengths: exploreByPattern's four-phase structure with named helper methods and matching phase comments makes the algorithm traceable without reading all 120 lines at once
executeBcqlQuery's two-phase split (collectHits / scoreHits) with per-phase HitRecord record is a clean pipeline pattern
SketchHandlers.executeRelationQueries centralises the loop-over-relations pattern with a BiFunction builder, eliminating two near-identical loops
CollocateProfileComparator.buildAdjectiveProfileList comments explicitly explain the two-pass trade-off for variance ('Second pass for variance — requires the mean — unavoidable')
Guard-clause-first dispatch in handleSketchRequest avoids nested if-else trees for the dep/surface/full branching
logic_clarity.issue_character: One isolated redundant computation in a hot path; no systemic control-flow problems.
logic_clarity.score_rationale: Logic throughout the codebase is clear and proportionate to the complexity of each operation. The algorithm classes are well-structured with named phases and focused helpers. The one findable clarity defect (redundant limit recomputation in scoreHits) is a minor local issue that a reader pauses on but resolves quickly. This fits the 90 anchor (mostly clear, isolated simplification opportunities) more than the 80 anchor (regularly obscures intent). Scored 86 because the redundant computation is in a frequently-read hot path and mildly erodes trust in the size-bounding logic.

strengths: jspecify @NonNull/@Nullable annotations are applied consistently to all public method parameters and return types in api/ and query/ — a developer can read the signature and trust the nullability contract
Optional<RelationType> in RelationConfig correctly models the absent case without overloading a nullable return
Sealed ConcordanceResult hierarchy enables exhaustive switch expressions without casting
CollocateQueryHelper.HitRecord.collocateLemma @Nullable annotation is accurate and matches the null-check pattern in every caller
ExplorationResult.empty(String) enforces its @NonNull intent via Objects.requireNonNull at the factory boundary
type_safety.issue_character: Two annotation gaps — one misleading @Nullable on a constructor parameter that must not be null in production, and one class whose fields lack the @NonNull annotations that the Javadoc promises — both isolated rather than systemic.
type_safety.score_rationale: The annotation discipline is strong in the api/ and query/ layers and is absent only in two specific places. The @Nullable-on-non-null-production-parameter defect is the more consequential one because it allows test harnesses that silently diverge from production behaviour. The ExplorationResult field gap is lower-stakes because the fields are private and the class already documents the guarantee in prose. Together these two issues push the score below 90 (generally accurate with soft spots) toward 83 — real friction but contained to two classes.

strengths: SemanticFieldExplorer correctly separates three different algorithm strategies (exploreByPattern, exploreMultiSeed, compareCollocateProfiles) into focused classes, with itself acting as a stable facade
ExplorationHandlers.parseExplorationParams and parseRelationConfig are precisely scoped helpers that do exactly one thing each
SketchHandlers.executeRelationQueries BiFunction builder pattern eliminates duplicated loop boilerplate across surface and dependency sketch paths
HttpApiUtils Javadoc explicitly justifies co-locating request parsing with response writing, signalling a deliberate design decision rather than an oversight
CollocateQueryHelper's two-phase pipeline (collectHits → scoreHits) with a named HitRecord intermediate type is a proportionate decomposition
design_coherence.issue_character: Two design issues in different subsystems — one is a wide positional constructor that makes construction sites opaque, the other is an open-ended Map parameter that defers typing indefinitely. Neither is systemic but both represent patterns that grow harder to audit as the codebase evolves.
design_coherence.score_rationale: Design coherence is strong in the exploration and query layers. Functions are focused, the algorithm decomposition is well-structured, and the facade pattern in SemanticFieldExplorer cleanly encapsulates complexity. The two defects (wide AdjectiveProfile constructor, open extraParams Map) are real costs to a developer modifying CollocateProfileComparator or adding a new explore parameter. They are not systemic but they are in high-traffic paths. This sits at 80 (solid but recurring friction): a developer mostly succeeds but hits one or two spots where they must count positional arguments or guess the Map contract.

strengths: model/ is a zero-dependency base layer — any package can import it without risk of introducing cycles
The api/ → exploration/ → query/ → model/ dependency chain is strictly downward with no skip-level or reverse edges
config/ correctly sits below api/ and exploration/ but above nothing application-specific — it is a shared leaf rather than a central hub
query/ does not import config/ — the grammar config is resolved at the api/exploration level and only patterns are passed down, keeping query/ reusable
utils/ has no application imports — pure functions that any layer can use without coupling to a domain module
cross_module_architecture.issue_character: A single test-level boundary inversion where a test in model/ imports production code from api/ — isolated and not present in any production code path.
cross_module_architecture.score_rationale: The production dependency graph is coherent and intentional: no cycles, no skip-level coupling, clear direction. The only boundary concern is at the test level where ExploreResponseAssemblerTest is placed in model/ but imports api/. This reflects genuine ownership ambiguity about where the assembler belongs (see also batch-11 finding) but does not affect production behaviour. At 88, the production architecture is solid (90 anchor) with the single test-boundary issue pulling it slightly below — consistent with 'mostly coherent with isolated boundary drift'.

strengths: WordSketchApiServer separates construction from port binding via start() — a developer can create the server, attach configuration, then bind, with no boot-order fragility
HttpApiUtils reads cors.allow.origin per call rather than caching at class load — test overrides via System.setProperty work reliably
GrammarConfigLoader.createDefaultEnglish() reads the grammar.config property per call, not at class load — the property can be set after class load and the loader will pick it up
No application-wide singletons or static mutable state outside of logger instances
BlackLabSnippetParser static Pattern constants are pure regex compilations with no environment or I/O dependencies
initialization_coupling.issue_character: One documented constructor I/O concern in BlackLabQueryExecutor — isolated, justified in the Javadoc, and mitigated by a test seam, but the mitigation introduces a @Nullable contract mismatch noted in batch-14.
initialization_coupling.score_rationale: Initialization coupling is minimal throughout: the server, config loader, and HTTP utilities are all designed to avoid class-load-time side effects and deferred I/O correctly. The only coupling is BlackLabQueryExecutor's constructor I/O, which is a deliberate and documented trade-off rather than an oversight. This fits the 90 anchor (mostly stable, limited fragility) with the constructor I/O pulling it to 89 — the trade-off is real but well-bounded.

strengths: Algorithm classes (SemanticFieldExplorer, MultiSeedExplorer, CollocateProfileComparator) have thorough unit tests using stub executors — the core logic is well-validated without a real index
ExploreResponseAssemblerTest validates edge building, response key population, and multi-seed edge sourcing — critical serialization logic is tested
VisualizationHandlersTest covers all four key paths (valid render, too-large body, invalid JSON, unknown mode) — good boundary coverage for a POST handler
GrammarConfigLoaderTest and RelationsValidationTest cover config parsing, validation errors, and alias resolution thoroughly
HttpApiUtilsTest and WrapHandlerTest cover the shared CORS, error-mapping, and method-enforcement infrastructure
test_strategy.issue_character: The test gap is concentrated in the SketchHandlers and ConcordanceHandlers happy paths — the API layer's most-used endpoints lack any test verifying that a valid request produces a structurally correct response, leaving response shape regressions undetectable without a live index.
test_strategy.score_rationale: The exploration and config test coverage is strong. The recent ExplorationHandlersTest and VisualizationHandlersTest additions improve handler coverage. However SketchHandlers — which handles the four main word-sketch endpoints — has no happy-path test at all: a developer refactoring handleFullSketchForType or the relation-response format has no unit-level regression signal. ConcordanceHandlers similarly. One of the three new ExplorationHandlers tests (comparison) is too weak to catch response-shape regressions. This collection of gaps is enough to score at 74 (a developer would worry about changes in certain areas) rather than 80 (strategic blind spots) — the sketch handler gap is both large and at the highest-traffic endpoint.

strengths: status: 'ok' / error: '...' envelope is applied uniformly across all endpoints — a consumer can write a single response checker
Explore endpoints consistently echo request parameters in a parameters sub-map, enabling request/response correlation without state
All count fields use a consistent {name}_count suffix — a consumer can predict what to look for
CORS headers are applied by every send method — consumers never receive a response without CORS headers
HTTP method enforcement (GET for exploration/sketch, POST for bcql/radial) is consistent and returns 405 with Allow header for mismatches
api_surface_coherence.issue_character: Two localised vocabulary inconsistencies at the boundary between similar-purpose endpoints — explore and compare endpoints use different vocabularies for the 'subject' concept (seed vs noun, relation_type presence), creating a small but real learning curve when a consumer uses both.
api_surface_coherence.score_rationale: The core envelope (status, error, parameters echo, count fields) is uniformly applied — a developer can predict the shape of any response from reading one example. The two issues are real but narrow: the relation_type omission in compare is cross-relational and intentional (noted in the Javadoc), and the adjective/noun vs seed/collocate vocabulary split affects only the two example endpoints. Neither causes functional breakage but both require a consumer to read two endpoint specs rather than inferring one from the other. This fits 83 — solid with recurring minor irregularities.

strengths: The no-auth security model is explicitly documented in WordSketchApiServer Javadoc with the rationale (loopback-only bind) and a deployment caveat
All 13 routes are registered through a single registerHandler path — there is no route that bypasses the CORS/method-check envelope
POST endpoints (bcql, radial) apply body size limits uniformly via readBodyWithSizeLimit, preventing resource exhaustion regardless of caller
BCQL complexity limits (length and bracket-depth) are applied before query execution — consistent defensive input validation
The loopback bind is hardcoded (InetAddress.getLoopbackAddress()), not configurable, making accidental external exposure impossible without a code change
authorization_consistency.issue_character: No auth inconsistencies exist — the no-auth design is uniform and intentional. The one minor gap is that the CORS origin deployment caveat does not mention the cors.allow.origin default as a related risk.
authorization_consistency.score_rationale: Authorization is uniformly not present — this is a documented, intentional design for a local research tool. All 13 endpoints apply the same (no-auth) treatment; there are no partially-protected resources or silently exposed routes. The loopback-only bind is hardcoded. The CORS default is restrictive for local use. The only concern is a deployment documentation gap (the Javadoc warns about bind address changes but omits the corresponding CORS default risk), which is minor. This is consistent with the 90 anchor: strong, mostly consistent with a limited documented exception.

issues.summary: CLAUDE.md code-structure map lists ~15 files that no longer exist, misdirecting new engineers
issues.suggestion: Update the CLAUDE.md '## Code Structure' section to reflect the current file tree. Run 'find src/main -name '*.java' | sort' and regenerate the tree, removing entries for deleted files and adding entries for newly introduced files such as exploration/CollocateProfileComparator.java, exploration/MultiSeedExplorer.java, config/RelationPatternBuilder.java, config/RelationUtils.java, model/ExplorationOptions.java, model/SingleSeedExplorationOptions.java, model/FetchExamplesOptions.java.
evidence: CLAUDE.md lists query/SemanticFieldExplorer.java — class is now in exploration/
CLAUDE.md lists query/QueryResults.java — class is now in model/

CLAUDE.md lists query/Token.java, query/TokenWindow.java, query/CQLVerifier.java, query/LemmaPredicate.java, query/TagPredicate.java, query/PosGroupPredicate.java and others — none of these files exist
CLAUDE.md lists api/PatternSubstitution.java — file does not exist in api/

issues.summary: ExploreResponseAssemblerTest lives in model/ test package but tests an api/ class
issues.suggestion: Move ExploreResponseAssemblerTest.java to src/test/java/pl/marcinmilkowski/word_sketch/api/ to co-locate it with the class under test and match the convention used by every other api/ test.
evidence: ExploreResponseAssemblerTest.java is under .../word_sketch/model/ in the test tree
Its import is 'import pl.marcinmilkowski.word_sketch.api.ExploreResponseAssembler' — the class being tested is in the api package
All other api/ tests (HandlersTest, ExplorationHandlersTest, VisualizationHandlersTest, etc.) live under .../word_sketch/api/ in the test tree

issues.summary: LogDiceUtils is 'public class' without 'final', breaking the utils/ static-utility convention
issues.suggestion: Change 'public class LogDiceUtils' to 'public final class LogDiceUtils' to match the convention established by all other utility classes in the codebase.
evidence: LogDiceUtils.java declares: 'public class LogDiceUtils {' — no 'final' modifier
MathUtils.java declares: 'public final class MathUtils {' with private constructor
CqlUtils.java declares: 'public final class CqlUtils {' with private constructor
RelationUtils.java declares: 'public final class RelationUtils {' with private constructor
RelationPatternBuilder.java declares: 'public final class RelationPatternBuilder {' with private constructor

issues.summary: CollocateProfileComparator.compareCollocateProfiles silently returns empty for null or undersized seed set instead of throwing
issues.suggestion: Replace the silent-return guard with a precondition throw: 'if (seedNouns == null || seedNouns.size() < 2) throw new IllegalArgumentException("compareCollocateProfiles requires at least 2 seed nouns");'. The HTTP layer already validates cardinality before calling the service, so this change only affects direct service callers (tests) and makes errors explicit.
evidence: compareCollocateProfiles starts with: 'if (seedNouns == null || seedNouns.size() < 2) { return ComparisonResult.empty(); }'
ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldComparison validates the same constraint and throws IllegalArgumentException: 'Comparison requires at least 2 seed nouns; received N'
A caller passing 1 seed to CollocateProfileComparator gets a silent empty result; passing the same 1 seed through the HTTP handler gets a 400 — inconsistent for the same business rule

issues.summary: SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern returns empty result for blank seed instead of throwing, making invalid input indistinguishable from no-results
issues.suggestion: Replace the silent return with: 'if (seed == null || seed.isBlank()) throw new IllegalArgumentException("seed must not be blank");'. The public overload (exploreByPattern(String, RelationConfig, SingleSeedExplorationOptions)) can add the same check before lowercasing. This makes invalid inputs audible at the point of call rather than producing a misleading 'no results found' response.
evidence: Package-private exploreByPattern(...) contains: 'if (seed.isEmpty()) { return ExplorationResult.empty(""); }'
ExplorationResult.empty() produces a result with no discovered nouns, no collocates — identical shape to a valid query that found nothing
No callers in the test tree construct an empty-seed call relying on this behavior — the empty check exists for defensive reasons but produces an ambiguous result

issues.summary: RelationConfig.computeDeprel() uses 'compute' for a pure field-derivation method that has no side effects and reads only from the record's own fields
issues.suggestion: Rename to 'deriveDeprel()' or 'extractDeprel()' to signal that this is a pure derivation from existing fields. Update all call sites in SketchHandlers (two calls in handleRelationsForType and handleDepSketchForType) and any references in tests.
evidence: computeDeprel() reads only this.pattern and this.id — both are immutable record components
The method applies a regex match and string prefix check to derive a deprel string — this is a derivation, not a computation over external state
The Javadoc says 'Extracts and computes the dependency relation' — 'extracts' is the primary verb, 'computes' is secondary and suggests more work than is done

issues.summary: ExplorationHandlers.parseRelationConfig does alias resolution + optional lookup + validation — 'parse' understates the method's compound responsibility
issues.suggestion: Rename to 'resolveRelationConfig' — 'resolve' accurately communicates alias lookup + config retrieval + validation as a single resolution step, consistent with how 'resolve' is used elsewhere (RelationUtils.resolveRelationAlias).
evidence: parseRelationConfig calls RelationUtils.resolveRelationAlias (alias resolution), then grammarConfig.getRelation (map lookup), then validates relationType is present — three distinct operations

'parse' in this codebase is used for JSON/string parsing (JSON.parseObject, parseBcqlRequest, parseConcordanceExamplesRequest) — the name implies a String→Object transformation from raw text, which is not what this method does
The method's @throws Javadoc correctly describes the validation behavior but the method name does not hint at it

issues.summary: ExploreResponseAssembler.buildEdges(ExplorationResult) creates cross-product SEED_ADJ edges in multi-seed mode, attributing all aggregate collocates to every seed
issues.suggestion: The fix requires ExplorationResult to carry per-seed collocate maps, or ExploreResponseAssembler to be given that data separately. The simplest approach: for multi-seed mode, generate SEED_ADJ edges from result.discoveredNouns() (each DiscoveredNoun is a seed, and its sharedCollocates() are the collocates actually found for it) rather than from result.seedCollocates(). Single-seed mode is unaffected since seeds() has one element and seedCollocates() is that seed's collocates. Add a seeds().size() > 1 branch in buildEdges to use discoveredNoun.sharedCollocates() for SEED_ADJ edges in multi-seed mode.
evidence: buildEdges loops: 'for (Map.Entry<String, Double> colloc : result.seedCollocates().entrySet()) { for (String seed : seedList) { edges.add(new Edge(seed, colloc.getKey(), ...)) } }'
In multi-seed mode, result.seedCollocates() is built by MultiSeedExplorer with 'seedCollocScores.merge(wsr.lemma(), wsr.logDice(), Math::max)' — a union across all seeds
The loop therefore produces (seedA, collocate_from_seedB) edges even when collocate_from_seedB did not appear for seedA

MultiSeedExplorer.buildDiscoveredNouns correctly tracks per-seed common collocates in the DiscoveredNoun objects, but buildEdges does not use discoveredNouns for SEED_ADJ edges — it uses the aggregate map instead

The Javadoc on buildEdges says 'each SEED_ADJ edge correctly names its source' — this is only true for which seed label appears on the edge, not for whether the collocate was actually found for that seed

issues.summary: ExploreResponseAssembler exposes internal format helpers as public static methods, widening the API surface beyond what external callers need
issues.suggestion: Reduce visibility of formatSeedCollocates, formatDiscoveredNouns, formatCoreCollocates, and serializeEdge to package-private (remove 'public'). If tests need to exercise them independently, test through the populate* entry points or move unit assertions to cover the aggregate output. formatAdjectiveProfile can remain public only if it has documented callers outside the package.
evidence: formatSeedCollocates, formatDiscoveredNouns, formatCoreCollocates are public static — they are called only from populateExploreResponse inside the same class
formatAdjectiveProfile is public static — called from populateComparisonResponse inside the same class, and from ExploreResponseAssemblerTest
serializeEdge is public static — called from populateExploreResponse and populateComparisonResponse inside the same class
The only callers outside the class are tests (ExploreResponseAssemblerTest), which could instead test through populateExploreResponse and populateComparisonResponse

issues.summary: VisualizationHandlers uses explicit null checks for width/height instead of the available fastjson2 default-value overload
issues.suggestion: Replace both null-check ternaries with: 'int width = obj.getIntValue("width", 840);' and 'int height = obj.getIntValue("height", 520);'. This is more direct and matches how center is handled elsewhere in the same method: 'if (center == null) center = "";' — even the string case would be cleaner as 'String center = obj.getString("center", "");'.
evidence: Code reads: 'int width = (obj.get("width") == null) ? 840 : obj.getIntValue("width");'
Code reads: 'int height = (obj.get("height") == null) ? 520 : obj.getIntValue("height");'
fastjson2's JSONObject provides getIntValue(String key, int defaultValue) — these two lines can each be reduced to a single call: 'int width = obj.getIntValue("width", 840);'

issues.summary: Redundant logger.isDebugEnabled() guard in CollocateProfileComparator.buildRawAdjectiveProfiles
issues.suggestion: Remove the isDebugEnabled() check; keep the isEmpty() check if subList allocation is a concern: 'if (!adjectives.isEmpty()) { logger.debug("  Top 5: {}", adjectives.subList(0, Math.min(5, adjectives.size()))); }'. Alternatively, if subList allocation is not a concern, simplify to: 'logger.debug("  Top 5: {}", adjectives.isEmpty() ? List.of() : adjectives.subList(0, Math.min(5, adjectives.size())));'
evidence: Code: 'if (!adjectives.isEmpty() && logger.isDebugEnabled()) { logger.debug("  Top 5: {}", adjectives.subList(0, Math.min(5, adjectives.size()))); }'
SLF4J's logger.debug() internally checks isDebugEnabled() before evaluating arguments in parameterized form
The isEmpty() check is a valid guard to skip the subList allocation, but isDebugEnabled() adds no additional protection since logger.debug() already does it
Other debug log calls in the same class use plain 'logger.debug(...)' without the isDebugEnabled() guard, making this an inconsistent outlier

issues.summary: ExplorationResult carries only an aggregate seedCollocates map, preventing ExploreResponseAssembler from producing accurate per-seed SEED_ADJ edges in multi-seed mode
issues.suggestion: Add a 'Map<String, Map<String, Double>> perSeedCollocates' field to ExplorationResult (empty map for single-seed mode; populated per seed for multi-seed mode). In MultiSeedExplorer.buildMultiSeedExplorationResult, populate it from the existing seedCollocateMap before returning. In ExploreResponseAssembler.buildEdges, use perSeedCollocates when it is non-empty to create (seed, collocate) edges only where the collocate was actually found for that seed.
evidence: ExplorationResult has a single 'Map<String, Double> seedCollocates' field — in multi-seed mode this is populated by MultiSeedExplorer with 'seedCollocScores.merge(wsr.lemma(), wsr.logDice(), Math::max)' (union across all seeds)

ExploreResponseAssembler.buildEdges loops 'for each collocate in result.seedCollocates(): for each seed in result.seeds(): add Edge(seed, collocate, ...)' — creates all-seeds × all-collocates cross-product

MultiSeedExplorer.SeedCollocateData internally holds the per-seed collocate map 'Map<String, List<WordSketchResult>> seedCollocateMap' but this is discarded after buildMultiSeedExplorationResult returns — the per-seed data does not survive the seam
The result: if collocate 'elegant' appears only for seed 'theory' (not 'model'), the edge (model, elegant, SEED_ADJ) is still created

issues.summary: LogDiceUtils class Javadoc uses a one-item 'Supported measures' list that implies extensibility never realised, a typical generative-boilerplate artifact
issues.suggestion: Simplify the class Javadoc to remove the list scaffolding: 'Utility class for computing the logDice collocation association score. Formula: log₂(2·f(AB) / (f(A) + f(B))) + 14, yielding values in the range 0–14.' This retains all useful content and removes the implies-extensibility framing.
evidence: Class Javadoc reads: 'Utility class for computing association scores for collocation analysis. Supported measures: - logDice: Symmetric association measure, range 0-14. Formula: log2(2 * f(AB) / (f(A) + f(B))) + 14'
There is only one method (compute) and only logDice is supported — the 'Supported measures:' list framing with a single bullet implies a catalogue that was never populated
The formula documentation is genuinely useful; the list structure is the artifact, not the content

CLAUDE.md lists 'query/QueryResults.java' — QueryResults is now in model/
