issues.summary: ExploreResponseAssembler is in api/ but has no HTTP dependencies; its test lives in the model/ test package
issues.suggestion: Move ExploreResponseAssembler to the model/ package (alongside ExplorationResult and ComparisonResult which it serves). Update the single import in ExplorationHandlers.java. Run: grep -r 'ExploreResponseAssembler' src/ to confirm all callsites, then move the file and update the package declaration. The test file already lives in the correct location.
evidence: ExploreResponseAssembler imports only from model/ and utils/ — no com.sun.net.httpserver, no HttpExchange
ExploreResponseAssemblerTest.java is in the .model test package, not .api, signalling that the test author considered it a model-layer concern
The class is declared public, used by ExplorationHandlers (api/), but contains pure Map-building logic with no HTTP contract

issues.summary: handleSemanticFieldComparison Javadoc says 'exactly 2 seeds' but code allows 2 or more
issues.suggestion: Update the Javadoc cardinality line in handleSemanticFieldComparison to 'requires at least 2 comma-separated seeds values' to match the actual guard. Change: 'Cardinality: requires exactly 2 comma-separated {@code seeds} values to form a meaningful pairwise comparison.' → 'Cardinality: requires at least 2 comma-separated {@code seeds} values; comparison is most meaningful with 2–4 seeds.'
evidence: Javadoc states: 'Cardinality: requires exactly 2 comma-separated seeds values to form a meaningful pairwise comparison'
Guard in code: 'if (seeds.size() < 2) throw new IllegalArgumentException(...)' — allows 3, 4, or more seeds through
The comparison algorithm in CollocateProfileComparator.compareCollocateProfiles handles any N >= 2 seeds correctly, so the code behaviour (N >= 2) is intentional

issues.summary: CollocateQueryHelper constructor annotates BlackLabIndex @Nullable but Javadoc says production callers must not pass null
issues.suggestion: Remove @Nullable and instead annotate with @VisibleForTesting or a custom comment to make the testing-only null contract explicit without misleading type-level annotation. Change: 'CollocateQueryHelper(@Nullable BlackLabIndex index)' → 'CollocateQueryHelper(BlackLabIndex index)' and add inline comment: '// null accepted only in test subclasses that override getTotalFrequency/executeCollocateSearch'
evidence: Constructor: 'CollocateQueryHelper(@Nullable BlackLabIndex index)' — @Nullable permits null at the type level
Javadoc: 'Null is accepted to support test subclasses that override getTotalFrequency and executeCollocateSearch. Production callers must always supply a non-null index.'

The annotation says one thing (null allowed), the prose says another (null only for test subclasses) — a consumer reading the signature alone sees a different contract than a consumer reading the Javadoc

issues.summary: scoreHits recomputes a limit from records.size() that is already bounded by sampleSize in the caller
issues.suggestion: Replace the limit computation in scoreHits with the simpler 'int limit = records.size()' and add a comment: '// records is already bounded to maxResults * OVER_FETCH_FACTOR by the caller'. This removes a confusing non-operative bound and clarifies where the size contract is established.
evidence: In executeBcqlQuery: 'int sampleSize = (int) Math.min(hits.size(), (long) maxResults * OVER_FETCH_FACTOR); ... List<HitRecord> hitRecords = collectHits(hits, sampleSize, ...)'
collectHits returns a list bounded to sampleSize: 'List<HitRecord> records = new ArrayList<>(sampleSize)'

In scoreHits: 'int limit = (int) Math.min((long) maxResults * OVER_FETCH_FACTOR, (long) records.size())' — because records.size() <= sampleSize <= maxResults*OVER_FETCH_FACTOR, limit is always records.size()
The Math.min is never the smaller branch, making the intent of the guard unclear to a reader

issues.summary: @Nullable on CollocateQueryHelper(BlackLabIndex) parameter contradicts the production non-null requirement
issues.suggestion: Remove @Nullable from the constructor parameter. The test-seam intent should be communicated via a comment or an @SuppressWarnings at the test subclass site, not by widening the production type contract. Change: 'CollocateQueryHelper(@Nullable BlackLabIndex index)' → 'CollocateQueryHelper(BlackLabIndex index)'. Add inline comment: '// null accepted only in test subclasses that override index-dependent methods'.
evidence: Constructor signature: 'CollocateQueryHelper(@Nullable BlackLabIndex index)'
Javadoc: 'Production callers must always supply a non-null index'

The @Nullable annotation tells static analysis tools and IDEs that null is a valid value, which contradicts the production intent and may suppress warnings at the only real callsite (BlackLabQueryExecutor) where a non-null index is always passed

issues.summary: ExplorationResult fields lack @NonNull despite Javadoc guaranteeing non-null collections
issues.suggestion: Add @NonNull (jspecify) to each non-null field and the corresponding accessors: annotate seeds, seedCollocates, seedCollocateFrequencies, discoveredNouns, and coreCollocates as @NonNull, and mirror on the accessor return types. This aligns the type annotations with the documented guarantee.
evidence: Javadoc: 'All collections are non-null; empty collections indicate the exploration found no candidates'
Fields 'private final List<String> seeds', 'private final Map<String, Double> seedCollocates', etc. carry no @NonNull annotations
Sibling model classes like AdjectiveProfile record have no annotations either, but ExplorationResult is a class with an explicit non-null contract in its Javadoc, making the gap more visible

issues.summary: AdjectiveProfile record has 10 positional double parameters making construction sites unreadable
issues.suggestion: Introduce a static factory or builder to group the computed statistics. The cleanest option is a nested AdjectiveStats record: 'record AdjectiveStats(int presentInCount, int totalNouns, double avgLogDice, double maxLogDice, double minLogDice, double variance, double commonalityScore, double distinctivenessScore)' and change AdjectiveProfile to '(String adjective, Map<String,Double> nounScores, AdjectiveStats stats)'. Update buildAdjectiveProfileList to build AdjectiveStats inline before constructing AdjectiveProfile.
evidence: AdjectiveProfile record: (adjective, nounScores, presentInCount, totalNouns, avgLogDice, maxLogDice, minLogDice, variance, commonalityScore, distinctivenessScore) — 10 components

Call site in buildAdjectiveProfileList: 'profiles.add(new AdjectiveProfile(adj, fullScores, presentIn, nounCount, avgScore, maxScore, minScore, variance, commonalityScore, distinctivenessScore))' — 7 positional double arguments with no names at the call site
A reviewer must count positions to verify that avgScore maps to avgLogDice and not maxLogDice; minScore and maxScore are adjacent doubles that are trivially transposable

issues.summary: buildCoreExploreResponse accepts an open Map<String,Object> extraParams with no type contract
issues.suggestion: Remove the extraParams Map and instead add a nullable Integer nounsPerCollocate parameter (or use Optional<Integer>): 'buildCoreExploreResponse(String relationType, int topCollocates, int minShared, double minLogDice, @Nullable Integer nounsPerCollocate)'. Inside the method: 'if (nounsPerCollocate != null) paramsUsed.put("nouns_per", nounsPerCollocate)'. This makes the type contract explicit and closes the open-ended extension point.
evidence: Signature: 'private Map<String,Object> buildCoreExploreResponse(String relationType, int topCollocates, int minShared, double minLogDice, Map<String,Object> extraParams)'
Currently called with: 'Map.of("nouns_per", exploreParams.nounsPerCollocate())' from single-seed path and 'Map.of()' from multi-seed path
The open Map parameter means any future caller can inject arbitrary keys into the parameters sub-map without the method knowing or validating them

issues.summary: ExploreResponseAssemblerTest lives in model/ test package but imports api/ production code
issues.suggestion: Move ExploreResponseAssembler to the model/ package (see also package_organization finding). This resolves the test boundary inversion and the production placement ambiguity simultaneously. Steps: (1) move ExploreResponseAssembler.java to model/, update package declaration; (2) update import in ExplorationHandlers.java; (3) ExploreResponseAssemblerTest already lives in the correct location. Run: grep -r 'import.*api.ExploreResponseAssembler' src/ to find all callsites before moving.
evidence: ExploreResponseAssemblerTest.java package: pl.marcinmilkowski.word_sketch.model
Import: 'import pl.marcinmilkowski.word_sketch.api.ExploreResponseAssembler'
This means the model/ test package depends on the api/ production package — upward in the dependency graph

issues.summary: SketchHandlers has no happy-path unit tests — full sketch, single-relation, and dep sketch response shapes are untested at the handler level
issues.suggestion: Add a SketchHandlersTest class following the ExplorationHandlersTest pattern: create a stub QueryExecutor returning canned WordSketchResult lists, invoke handleSketchRequest (full sketch), handleRelationSketch, and handleFullDependencySketch, and assert: status=200, response contains 'status'='ok', 'lemma' is echoed, 'relations' is a non-null map. This verifies response shape without a real index. Three tests covering the main dispatch branches would provide meaningful regression protection.
evidence: HandlersTest tests only the missing-lemma 400 path for SketchHandlers — no test constructs a SketchHandlers with a stub executor and verifies the response body
handleFullSketchForType, handleRelationSketch, handleFullDependencySketch, and handleRelationQueryForPattern all have zero happy-path test coverage at the handler level
ExplorationHandlersTest shows the pattern for adding stub-executor handler tests — the same approach applies to SketchHandlers

issues.summary: handleSemanticFieldComparison test only asserts body != null, missing response structure verification
issues.suggestion: Strengthen the comparison test: after parsing the body, assert: 'assertEquals("ok", body.getString("status"))', 'assertNotNull(body.getJSONArray("adjectives"))', 'assertNotNull(body.getJSONArray("seeds"))', 'assertNotNull(body.getJSONObject("parameters"))'. These three assertions mirror the level of coverage the other two exploration handler tests provide.
evidence: Test: 'assertNotNull(body, "Response body should be valid JSON")' — no assertion on 'status', 'seeds', 'adjectives', or 'parameters' keys
Sister tests (handleSemanticFieldExplore and handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti) both assert on specific response fields
The comparison endpoint has a different response shape (no relation_type, different top-level keys) that could regress silently with this minimal assertion

issues.summary: /api/concordance/examples and /api/semantic-field/examples use different keys for equivalent noun/adjective roles
issues.suggestion: Standardise on 'noun'/'adjective' across both example endpoints. In ConcordanceHandlers.handleConcordanceExamples change: 'response.put("seed", req.noun())' → 'response.put("noun", req.noun())' and 'response.put("collocate", req.adjective())' → 'response.put("adjective", req.adjective())'. The HTTP params (seed/collocate) can remain as they are — only the response body keys need to change. Update the Javadoc comment in ConcordanceHandlers to reflect the new response keys.
evidence: /api/concordance/examples response: 'response.put("seed", req.noun())' and 'response.put("collocate", req.adjective())' — HTTP params are seed/collocate
/api/semantic-field/examples response: 'response.put("adjective", adjective)' and 'response.put("noun", noun)' — HTTP params are adjective/noun
Both endpoints return concordance examples for an adjective-noun pair; a frontend developer consuming both must map seed→noun, collocate→adjective and vice versa

issues.summary: /api/semantic-field/compare response omits relation_type present in sibling explore endpoints without documenting the omission in the response contract
issues.suggestion: Add a note to the response contract in the handleSemanticFieldComparison Javadoc: '@apiNote The response intentionally omits {@code relation_type} because comparison aggregates across all loaded relations rather than filtering to one.' Alternatively, include 'relation_type': 'cross_relational' in the compare response body to give consumers a uniform key with a sentinel value. The documentation fix is lower-risk; the sentinel value approach is more consumer-friendly.
evidence: explore and explore-multi responses include 'response.put("relation_type", relationType)' at the top level
compare response (populateComparisonResponse) does not include 'relation_type' — intentional because it is cross-relational
The Javadoc of handleSemanticFieldComparison explains why there is no relation parameter, but does not mention that the response omits relation_type
A frontend developer seeing relation_type in explore responses and building a unified response parser would need to add a special case for compare

issues.summary: WordSketchApiServer deployment warning mentions bind address but omits the cors.allow.origin default risk for container deployments
issues.suggestion: Extend the deployment warning in WordSketchApiServer Javadoc to mention CORS: 'Any change to the bind address must be accompanied by an authentication or network-level access-control layer. Also set -Dcors.allow.origin=<trusted-origin> to restrict cross-origin access to the intended client origin.' This keeps the security guidance co-located with the bind-address note.
evidence: WordSketchApiServer Javadoc: 'Any change to the bind address must be accompanied by an authentication or network-level access-control layer' — does not mention CORS
HttpApiUtils: 'private static final String DEFAULT_CORS_ALLOW_ORIGIN = "http://localhost:3000"' — the default is narrowly scoped to localhost:3000
A developer following the deployment warning who changes the bind address to 0.0.0.0 might not realise the CORS default also needs to change for a multi-origin container setup
